questions.text: What is the main theme of Surah Saba regarding Allah's ownership and control?
questions.answer: That Allah alone owns and governs the heavens and the earth, and all outcomes return to Him.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches that Allah's praise is limited to worldly blessings only.
questions.answer: False. It connects Allah's praise to both this world and the Hereafter.

questions.text: How does Surah Saba correct the excuse: 'We only worship intermediaries to get closer to Allah'?
questions.answer: It teaches that worship belongs to Allah alone, and excuses do not justify associating partners with Him.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about Allah's knowledge of hidden intentions and secret actions?
questions.answer: Allah knows what is concealed and what is revealed; nothing is hidden from Him.

questions.text: Yes/No: Does the Surah portray disbelief in resurrection as a serious spiritual disease?
questions.answer: Yes. It presents denial of the Hereafter as rooted in arrogance and doubt, leading to loss.

questions.text: What is the relationship between belief in the Hereafter and taking personal responsibility?
questions.answer: Belief in the Hereafter pushes a person to own their choices and prepare for accountability.

questions.text: What lesson does the Surah teach about receiving blessings through the examples of Dawud and Sulayman?
questions.answer: Great blessings and power are tests that require humility, gratitude, and obedience to Allah.

questions.text: Why is the story of Dawud's armor-making spiritually significant?
questions.answer: It shows that worship includes responsible effort, excellence in work, and serving people with what Allah grants.

questions.text: What does the Surah imply about the limits of created beings like jinn?
questions.answer: They are powerful creatures but do not know the unseen and remain under Allah's control.

questions.text: How does the Surah respond to the misconception that jinn can reveal the unseen reliably?
questions.answer: It demonstrates that jinn did not even know a major reality right before them, proving their limitation.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about gratitude (shukr) as an action, not just a feeling?
questions.answer: Shukr is shown by obedience, avoiding sin, and using blessings in ways that please Allah.

questions.text: True/False: Ingratitude is presented as harmless as long as someone remains wealthy and successful.
questions.answer: False. The Surah shows ingratitude can lead to ruin even after great prosperity.

questions.text: What is the main moral warning in the story of the people of Saba?
questions.answer: That comfort without gratitude and obedience can turn into hardship and destruction.

questions.text: How can a community protect blessings according to the Surah's lessons?
questions.answer: By being grateful, just, obedient to Allah, and not becoming arrogant with prosperity.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about asking Allah for hardship when He has given ease?
questions.answer: It is self-oppression and a sign of ingratitude; it can lead to real hardship and regret.

questions.text: What is one way Satan misleads people according to the Surah's message?
questions.answer: By making false beliefs and sinful lifestyles seem reasonable until it is too late.

questions.text: Does Satan have the power to force a person to disbelieve? Explain briefly.
questions.answer: No. He only invites and whispers; people choose whether to follow him.

questions.text: What does Surah Saba teach about blind following in religion and culture?
questions.answer: Blind following is not an excuse; people are responsible for seeking truth and not following misguidance.

questions.text: Why will excuses and blame-shifting fail on the Day of Judgment?
questions.answer: Because Allah judges with perfect knowledge, and each person is accountable for what they chose.

questions.text: What is the Surah's warning about wealth and children as a measure of Allah's love?
questions.answer: They are not proof of honor; true honor is faith and righteous deeds.

questions.text: How does the Surah balance between hope and fear for the listener?
questions.answer: It warns of consequences for denial and ingratitude while inviting reflection, repentance, and sincere faith.

questions.text: What is one proof that truth is stronger than falsehood in this Surah?
questions.answer: Truth remains and clarifies reality, while falsehood collapses and cannot create or restore anything lasting.

questions.text: What does the Surah suggest a sincere seeker should do when doubts arise?
questions.answer: Stand sincerely for Allah and reflect calmly and fairly rather than rejecting out of pride.

questions.text: True/False: People can delay faith until the moment they see punishment and still be safe.
questions.answer: False. The Surah warns that delayed faith may be blocked when punishment arrives.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about the difference between worldly regret and repentance?
questions.answer: Worldly regret after consequences is not the same as sincere repentance before the end.

questions.text: Why is gratitude described as rare among people?
questions.answer: Because it requires humility and consistent obedience, which many neglect despite enjoying blessings.

questions.text: How does Surah Saba teach that Allah's tests can come through both ease and hardship?
questions.answer: Prosperity tests gratitude and humility, while hardship tests patience and faith.

questions.text: What does the Surah imply about the relationship between truth and reflection?
questions.answer: Truth becomes clearer through sincere reflection, while refusal to reflect keeps a person trapped in falsehood.

questions.text: What is one life application lesson from the story of Saba about infrastructure and security?
questions.answer: Material strength does not protect without gratitude and righteousness; Allah can remove security when people persist in wrongdoing.

questions.text: How does Surah Saba address the idea that prophets are delusional or mentally unstable?
questions.answer: It invites people to reflect sincerely and recognize the Prophet's soundness and the clarity of his message.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about the fairness of Allah's judgment?
questions.answer: Allah judges with full knowledge and perfect justice; no one will be wronged.

questions.text: What is the purpose of warnings in this Surah?
questions.answer: To establish proof, awaken hearts, and give people a chance to return to Allah before the Hereafter.

questions.text: How does this Surah define true success?
questions.answer: Success is faith and righteous deeds that lead to Allah's pleasure and the reward of the Hereafter.

questions.text: True/False: A person's social rank can protect them from Allah's accountability.
questions.answer: False. Status does not save anyone; each person stands before Allah.

questions.text: What does Surah Saba teach about the danger of arrogance when confronted with guidance?
questions.answer: Arrogance blocks reflection and leads to denial, even when the truth is clear.

questions.text: How can a person increase their chances of being among the grateful servants?
questions.answer: By regularly recognizing Allah's favors, thanking Him, and consistently obeying Him with humility.

questions.text: What is a key spiritual takeaway from the Surah's ending about wanting faith too late?
questions.answer: Do not delay repentance; guidance should be accepted while there is time and opportunity.

questions.text: How does Surah Saba teach that truth and falsehood affect daily life, not only theology?
questions.answer: Believing truth shapes gratitude, morals, and choices, while falsehood leads to injustice, denial, and eventual regret.

questions.text: What does Surah Saba teach about contentment (qana'ah) in contrast to always wanting changes?
questions.answer: It encourages appreciating Allah's ease and provision instead of rejecting it out of boredom or arrogance.

questions.text: What is one way to avoid becoming like those who argued and blamed others in the Hereafter?
questions.answer: Seek knowledge, follow evidence, and refuse to follow leaders or crowds when they contradict Allah's guidance.

questions.text: What is the Surah's message about worship and reliance (tawakkul)?
questions.answer: Rely on Allah while taking lawful means, and keep worship purely for Him without partners.

questions.text: Why is it important to reflect alone or with someone trustworthy when exploring faith?
questions.answer: It reduces social pressure and helps a person think honestly, increasing the chance of accepting truth.

questions.text: How does Surah Saba teach that guidance is a mercy?
questions.answer: Allah sends reminders and warnings so people can correct themselves and avoid the regret of the Hereafter.

questions.text: What does the Surah imply about the stability of false beliefs over time?
questions.answer: False beliefs are unstable and eventually collapse, even if they seem strong for a while.

questions.text: What is one lesson about humility from the prophets mentioned in this Surah?
questions.answer: Even with great power and miracles, prophets remained devoted, grateful, and obedient to Allah.

questions.text: What does Surah Saba teach about using travel, trade, and provision ethically?
questions.answer: Use ease and provision with gratitude and justice, and do not let prosperity lead to sin or arrogance.

questions.text: What is a misconception about 'gratefulness' that this Surah corrects?
questions.answer: That gratitude is only saying thanks; the Surah shows it must be proven by deeds and obedience.

questions.text: Why does the Surah repeatedly remind that Allah's knowledge is complete?
questions.answer: To strengthen faith, encourage accountability, and remove the illusion that wrongdoing can remain hidden.

questions.text: What is the overall purpose of Surah Saba for someone studying it seriously?
questions.answer: To deepen tawhid, cultivate gratitude, warn against arrogance and denial, and prepare for the Hereafter.

questions.text: What is one final takeaway about choosing between truth and falsehood in Surah Saba?
questions.answer: Truth is firm and beneficial, while falsehood is weak and ends in regret; accept truth early and live by it.

questions.text: How does the Surah begin regarding praise?
questions.answer: All praise is for Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and to Him belongs praise in the Hereafter.

questions.text: What was the unique miracle given to Prophet Dawud (David) regarding the mountains and birds?
questions.answer: They were commanded to repeat Allah's praises along with him.

questions.text: What material was made 'pliable' (soft) for Dawud (AS)?
questions.answer: Iron, so he could make coats of armor.

questions.text: What was the speed of the wind subjected to Sulayman (AS)?
questions.answer: Its morning journey was a month and its afternoon journey was a month.

questions.text: What was the 'fountain' given to Sulayman?
questions.answer: A fountain of molten copper.

questions.text: How did the Jinn find out that Sulayman (AS) had died?
questions.answer: They only realized he was dead when a creature of the earth (termite) ate his staff, and he fell down.

questions.text: What lesson did the Jinn learn from the death of Sulayman?
questions.answer: That if they had known the Unseen, they would not have continued in the humiliating punishment (their labor).

questions.text: What was the 'Sign' for the people of Saba (Sheba) in their homeland?
questions.answer: Two gardens, one on the right and one on the left, in a land that was good and fertile.

questions.text: How were the people of Saba punished for their ungratefulness?
questions.answer: Allah sent upon them the flood of the dam (Arim) and replaced their lush gardens with gardens of bitter fruit, tamarisks, and some lote trees.

questions.text: What was the specific 'wish' of the people of Saba that led to their downfall?
questions.answer: They asked Allah to make the distance between their journeys longer, thus acting unjustly to themselves.

questions.text: Does Iblis (Satan) have any authority over mankind?
questions.answer: No, he has no authority; his influence is only to distinguish those who believe in the Hereafter from those who are in doubt.

questions.text: What happens to the 'argument' between the weak and the arrogant on the Day of Judgment?
questions.answer: The weak will blame the arrogant for misleading them, and the arrogant will say they only followed what was presented to them; both will be in punishment.

questions.text: Can wealth and children bring one closer to Allah?
questions.answer: No, except for those who believe and do righteous deeds; for them is a manifold reward.

questions.text: What does the Surah declare about the arrival of truth and the fate of falsehood?
questions.answer: It declares that truth has come, and falsehood can neither begin anything nor restore what it loses.

questions.text: What is the Surah's single advice to skeptics who doubt the Prophet's message?
questions.answer: To stand up for Allah, in pairs and individually, and then reflect that the Prophet is not mad.

questions.text: What is the 'great distance' mentioned in the the closing ayah?
questions.answer: The distance between the disbelievers and the faith they wish to have once the punishment arrives; it will be too late.

questions.text: Who are described as 'few' among Allah's servants in this Surah?
questions.answer: The grateful ones (Ash-Shakur).

questions.text: Does Allah know the weight of an atom in the heavens or earth?
questions.answer: Yes, nothing escapes Him, not even something smaller or larger than that.

questions.text: What will the disbelievers say when they are presented with the punishment?
questions.answer: They will try to believe, but a barrier will be placed between them and what they desire.

questions.text: What is the 'Saba' mentioned in the title?
questions.answer: A kingdom in ancient Yemen known for its prosperity and eventual destruction due to disbelief.
